steps.text: Evaluate physical security measures, including a thorough review of the building(s), premises, laboratories, and biological material storage areas.
Limit access to authorized and designated employees based on the need to enter sensitive areas.
Evaluate levels of access, considering all facets of the laboratory's operations and programs.
Conduct periodic reviews of staff to maintain a personnel reliability and violence prevention plan.
Establish an anonymous peer and threat reporting system.
Institute an Employee Health and Wellness Program.
Foster leadership accountability to address submitted reports.
Develop policies for personnel and visitor identification, visitor management, access procedures, and reporting of security incidents.
Establish material accountability procedures to track the inventory, storage, use, transfer, destruction, and inactivation of biological materials.
Define the materials (or forms of materials) subject to accountability measures.

constraints.text: The effectiveness of a laboratory biosecurity program depends on the integrity and awareness of individuals who have access to pathogens, toxins, sensitive information, and/or other assets.

preconditions.text: Importation of this agent requires CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
Domestic transport of this agent may require a permit from USDA APHIS VS.
A DoC permit may be required for the export of this agent to another country.

constraints.text: Parenteral inoculation and infectious aerosols are the primary potential laboratory hazards associated with handling infectious clinical materials.
Selection of an appropriate disinfectant is an important consideration for laboratories working with mycobacteria.
BSL-2 practices, containment equipment, and facilities are recommended for all activities with known or potentially infectious materials from humans and animals.
It is recommended to use extraordinary care to avoid accidental parenteral inoculation with contaminated sharp instruments.
ABSL-2 practices, containment equipment, and facilities are recommended for animal studies utilizing rodents, armadillos, and NHPs.

M. tuberculosis and M. bovis infections are a proven hazard to laboratory personnel and others who may be exposed to infectious aerosols in the laboratory, autopsy rooms, and other healthcare facilities.
Multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug-resistant (XDR) strains are of particular concern.

steps.text: Strategically place squirt bottles containing 70% ethanol and hand sanitizer bottles around the laboratory to facilitate immediate access after accidental spills/exposures.
Kill snails and cercariae in the water of laboratory aquaria using ethanol, bleach, or by heating the water to ≥50°C.
Discharge the treated water to sanitary sewers.
Import any of these agents.
Transport these agents domestically.
Export these agents to another country.

preconditions.text: CDC and/or USDA importation permits are obtained.
A permit from USDA APHIS VS is obtained (if required).
A Department of Commerce (DoC) permit is obtained (if required).

constraints.text: Use BSL-2 and ABSL-2 practices, including appropriate PPE and containment equipment/facilities, for laboratory work with infective stages of trematode parasites.
Wear gloves, long-sleeved laboratory coats, and face shields or other protective garb when working in the immediate area of aquaria or other water sources that may contain schistosome cercariae.

steps.text: Provide specific, regular training for B virus hazards, including understanding the modes of exposure and transmission, to individuals encountering B virus hazards.
Include proper use of engineering controls and personal protective equipment in the training.
Perform immediate and thorough cleansing following bites, scratches, splashes, or contact with potential fomites in high-risk areas.

preconditions.text: A bite, scratch, splash, or contact with potential fomites in high-risk areas occurs.

constraints.text: Practice barrier precautions and rapid and thorough cleansing immediately following possible site contamination.
Take precautions when work requires the use of any macaque species, even antibody-negative animals.
Use personal protective equipment, which is essential to prevention.

steps.text: Conduct a risk assessment for chimeric viruses, evaluating replication ability, altered virulence, intracranial virulence/attenuation patterns, and lack of reversion to virulence.
Provide a clear description of the strains involved to accompany the risk assessment.

preconditions.text: Prior to assigning Biosafety Levels and containment practices for chimeric viruses.

constraints.text: Do not assign Biosafety Levels to hybrid or chimeric strains based solely on parental types; a separate risk assessment must be completed due to potential altered biohazard potential.

steps.text: Consider additional economic and environmental risks when developing biosafety and biocontainment risk assessments for working with pathogens that infect agricultural animals.
Distinguish between agricultural pathogens and pathogens that are solely a threat to human health when implementing biocontainment guidelines and risk mitigation strategies.

preconditions.text: Staff must receive species-specific training in animal behavior, effective handling practices, and other physical safety precautions before working with large animals.

constraints.text: Agricultural animals can cause injuries and/or physical trauma to workers due to their size and disposition.

steps.text: Design HVAC system pressure differentials to incorporate engineering features that protect against sustained reversal of directional airflow.

preconditions.text: A site-specific risk assessment must be conducted.

constraints.text: Protect against sustained reversal of directional airflow in the event of a breach of containment (e.g., opening doors).

steps.text: Restrict treatments to areas of known pest activity.
Apply pesticides using the least toxic product(s) available and in the most effective and safe manner.
Perform quality assurance and program review to evaluate IPM activities and effectiveness.
Modify current IPM protocols and implement new procedures based on the review.

preconditions.text: Pest management personnel must be licensed and certified by the appropriate regulatory agency(s).

constraints.text: Fogging should be avoided.
Limit the scope of pesticide treatments to minimize the potential of pesticide exposure to the research environment and staff.

steps.text: Maintain inactivation equipment and procedures regularly to ensure consistent inactivation efficacy.
Provide regular safety training on current, modified, or new inactivation and verification procedures to all affected staff.
Conduct re-training to emphasize lessons learned from the root cause analysis.
Perform in-house testing for all inactivation methodologies.
Include inactivation and verification data in the 'Materials and Methods' sections of publications.

constraints.text: Chemical and physical hazards of inactivation procedures must be regularly assessed as part of the routine review.
Refer to OSHA's Laboratory Safety Guidance for regulations and guidance on handling hazardous materials.
